coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-adorned silver nanoparticles as a straightforward sustainable colorimetric sensor for the rapid detection of ketotifen in eye drops and aqueous humor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Saad, Aya Magdy
El-Deen, Asmaa Kamal
Nasr, Jenny Jeehan Mohamed
coredata.dc:description: The rapid and accurate detection of drug molecules in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples is of paramount importance. In this research article, we present a novel colorimetric sensor based on carbon dots decorated silver nanoparticles (CDs/AgNPs) for the rapid detection of ketotifen (KTF), a widely used antihistamine drug. The CDs were synthesized via a facile one-step microwave-assisted method and subsequently conjugated onto AgNPs through a simple adsorption process, forming a stable CDs/AgNPs composite. The resulting composite exhibited unique optical properties, including a strong absorption peak at 410 nm with remarkable intensity reduction and color changes upon the addition of KTF. The developed colorimetric sensor exhibited a wide linear range of 3.0–40.0 µg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9996), with a %RSD of 2.41, and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.981 µg mL−1. Furthermore, the sensor's practical applicability was evaluated by successfully detecting KTF in eye drops and artificial aqueous humor, demonstrating a remarkable percentage recovery exceeding 96.0 %. Finally, a comprehensive evaluation of the greenness and blueness of the method was performed using analytical eco-scale, GAPI, AGREEprep, and BAGI tools. The results of these assessments indicate its exceptional sustainability. Overall, the proposed method holds significant potential for applications in pharmaceutical quality control and therapeutic monitoring, contributing to improved patient care and drug safety in the field of ophthalmology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ketotifen
Carbon dots
Silver nanoparticles
Colorimetric sensor
Eye drops
Aqueous humor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs adorned silver nanoparticles (CDs/AgNPs)
General procedure for the determination of KTF
Optimization of the experimental conditions for KTF sensing
Mechanism of colorimetric sensing of KTF
Comparison of the developed method with other reported methods for KTF determination
Comprehensive evaluation of the method greenness and blueness
Assessment of the method's greenness profile using analytical Eco-Scale, ComplexGAPI, and AGREEprep tools
Assessment of the method's blueness based on BAGI tool

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phar-23\u201340).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the procedure for preparation of CDs/AgNPs.
Characterization of the prepared CDs/AgNPs where: (a) FTIR spectrum of CDs/AgNPs and (b) TEM images of CDs/AgNPs at different magnification powers.
UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) CDs, (b) CDs/AgNPs (c) CDs/AgNPs/KTF.
Assessment of the greenness of the developed method using (a) Analytical Eco-scale, (b) ComplexGAPI, (c) AGREEprep, and (d) BAGI tools.
Analytical performance data for the determination of KTF by the proposed method.
Precision data for the determination of KTF by the proposed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each reading is the average of three replicates.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed method for the determination of KTF in different matrices.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each reading is the average of three replicates.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the developed method with other reported method for KTF analysis in different matrices.

row.entry:
- C18 column (4.6 x 150 mm, 5 μm) at 40° C
- mobile phase is a mixture of triethylamine and water and methanol
The method is based on the catalytic effect of KTF in the CL reaction of tris (1,10 phenanthroline) ruthenium (II), with Ce(IV) in sulfuric acid.
This method is based on the reaction with bromocresol purple to form yellow complex measured at 399 nm
This method is based on quenching the continuous fluorescence of 9(10H)-Acridone
Eye drops and artificial aqueous humor
This method is based on reducing the intensity of the surface plasmon resonance of CDs/AgNPs at 410 nm upon reaction with KTF.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-adorned silver nanoparticles as a straightforward sustainable colorimetric sensor for the rapid detection of ketotifen in eye drops and aqueous humor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura National University, Gamasa 7731168, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura National University, Gamasa 7731168, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, 35516, Egypt.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid and accurate detection of drug molecules in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples is of paramount importance. In this research article, we present a novel colorimetric sensor based on carbon dots decorated silver nanoparticles (CDs/AgNPs) for the rapid detection of ketotifen (KTF), a widely used antihistamine drug. The CDs were synthesized via a facile one-step microwave-assisted method and subsequently conjugated onto AgNPs through a simple adsorption process, forming a stable CDs/AgNPs composite. The resulting composite exhibited unique optical properties, including a strong absorption peak at 410 nm with remarkable intensity reduction and color changes upon the addition of KTF. The developed colorimetric sensor exhibited a wide linear range of 3.0–40.0 µg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9996), with a %RSD of 2.41, and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.981 µg mL−1. Furthermore, the sensor's practical applicability was evaluated by successfully detecting KTF in eye drops and artificial aqueous humor, demonstrating a remarkable percentage recovery exceeding 96.0 %. Finally, a comprehensive evaluation of the greenness and blueness of the method was performed using analytical eco-scale, GAPI, AGREEprep, and BAGI tools. The results of these assessments indicate its exceptional sustainability. Overall, the proposed method holds significant potential for applications in pharmaceutical quality control and therapeutic monitoring, contributing to improved patient care and drug safety in the field of ophthalmology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ketotifen (KTF) is an antihistamine drug that works by preventing the release of histamine, a chemical in the body that triggers allergic reactions. It is an organic heterotricyclic molecule that is 4,9-dihydro-10H-benzo [4,5] cyclohepta[1,2-b]thiophen-10-one with a 1-methylpiperidin-4-ylidene group replaced at position 4 [Fig. 1 ]. It is a mast cell stabilizer and histamine H1 receptor blocker used to treat mild atopic asthma and allergic conjunctivitis [1]. It is an asthma treatment that, when used daily alongside other anti-asthmatic medications, can effectively reduce the frequency, severity, and duration of asthma symptoms or attacks in children. Its use may potentially help lessen the need for additional daily anti-asthmatic medications. As a result, the accurate detection of KTF in pharmaceuticals and biological fluids is of utmost importance. Various analytical methods have been reported in the literature for this purpose, including liquid chromatography [2–9], capillary electrophoresis [10,11], electrochemistry [12–16], turbidimetry [17], spectrophotometry [18–22], spectrofluorimetry [23,24], and chemiluminescence [25]. These conventional methods often involve complex procedures, time-consuming sample preparation, and expensive instrumentation [26]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for the development of simple, cost-effective, and rapid detection methods for KTF.

Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nanoparticles characterized by their minute diameters, measured in nanometers. They display remarkable physicochemical properties, including photoluminescence that varies with size and wavelength, resistance to photobleaching, and ease of bioconjugation [27]. They have emerged as a promising class of nanomaterials for various analytical applications due to their unique optical properties, excellent stability, and biocompatibility [27–29]. CDs could interact with analytes through the surface functionalization [30,31]. Furthermore, their small size and large surface area make them ideal candidates for sensitive and selective detection of target molecules. Integration of CDs with other nanomaterials can further enhance their sensing capabilities and expand their scope of applications [32–36].

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have attracted the interest of researchers due to their unique and attainable optical features derived from the local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [37–40]. Spherical AgNPs, typically measuring around 10 nm, exhibit a significant LSPR absorption band with a maximum of around 390 ∼ 420 nm [41]. The precise position of this peak is determined by both the size and form of the NPs as well as their local dielectric environment. Because of electromagnetic retardation in larger particles, the LSPR peak tends to shift towards longer wavelengths (a phenomenon known as red-shifting) as the size of the NPs increases [42]. Such LSPR-based optical phenomena have served as a foundation for the development of several remarkable sensors and biosensors [43,44]. Concurrently, several materials were employed both as reducing agents and stabilizers during the manufacture of AgNPs. Among these, carbon-based nanomaterials are particularly intriguing and have been the subject of a great deal of research in recent years [45,46]. By combining CDs with AgNPs, a synergistic effect can be generated, leading to improved sensing performance and enhanced signal transduction [45–48] CDs have recently been employed as reducing agents in the synthesis of metal nanoparticles for sensing applications. Wang et al. [49], for example, used CDs to create Ag@Carbon dot composites and measured glucose during the synthesis process. Moreover, Shen et al. [50] used CDs as reducing agents in the development of AgNPs and created an optical probe for biothiol assays using the produced NPs. The researchers Chen and colleagues [51] described a novel method for growing AgNPs on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and used them for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose. Furthermore, Shi et al. [52] developed a colorimetric approach for discriminating dihydroxybenzene isomers using nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) as a reducing agent and stabilizer for the synthesis of N-GQDs/AgNPs hybrid. Despite their numerous advantages, the synthesis procedures for most of these techniques typically necessitate the use of high temperatures and/or prolonged reaction times.

Consequently, this study aims to develop a sustainable, simple, and straightforward approach for synthesizing CDs/AgNPs and applying them as a reliable colorimetric sensor for the quantification of KTF in both eye drops and aqueous humor. This innovative method overcomes the limitations associated with conventional detection methods. The study employs CDs as stabilizers and reducing agents to synthesize AgNPs. By adorning the AgNPs with CDs, the sensor benefits from the unique properties and functionalities of both materials, enhancing the sensitivity and selectivity for KTF detection. The developed sensor exhibits a significant color alteration in the presence of KTF, enabling straightforward visual interpretation. This colorimetric readout simplifies the detection process, eliminating the need for complex instrumentation, and promoting user-friendliness. Furthermore, the study successfully applies the developed sensor to quantify KTF concentration in both eye drops and artificial aqueous humor. This successful application demonstrates the sensor's potential for use in pharmaceutical quality control and therapeutic monitoring within the field of ophthalmology.

Chemicals of Analytical Grade and solvents of HPLC grade were used. Standard KTF (purity of 99.7 %) was kindly provided by Orchidia Pharmaceutical Industries, Ltd, Egypt. Acetic acid, ammonia, sodium acetate, sodium hydroxide, glucose, sodium chloride, dextrose monohydrate, dibasic sodium phosphate, sodium bicarbonate, and boric acid, were purchased from El Nasr Chemical Co., Egypt. Methanol (HPLC grade), and silver nitrate, were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Seelze, Germany). Calcium chloride dihydrate, Potassium chloride, and magnesium chloride hexahydrate were purchased from Nile Chemical Co., Egypt. Orchazid® eyedrops (Batch #100822150), manufactured by Orchidia Pharmaceutical Industries and labeled to contain 0.345 mg of KTF/1.0 mL drops were purchased from an Egyptian pharmacy. Distilled water was used throughout the whole study.

The spectroscopic measurements were conducted using a Shimadzu UV-1601 PC (Kyoto, Japan), a UV–visible double-beam spectrophotometer equipped with matched 1.0 cm pathlength quartz cells. The pH values were adjusted using a pH meter (Consort, P-901) from Belgium. For stirring, a magnetic stirrer (Daihan Scientific CO, South Korea) was employed. Temperature control was maintained using a water bath from England Cambridge Ltd.

The CDs were synthesized from glucose using a microwave-assisted technique as in a previous study [53] with slight modification. Initially, 30.0 mL of a 5.0 % aqueous glucose solution was placed in a covered glass container and subjected to heating in a typical microwave oven operating at 1400 W for 10.0 min. Subsequently, the residue turns yellow, indicating the successful formation of CDs. The resulting CDs were then adjusted to a volume of 30.0 mL using deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs adorned silver nanoparticles (CDs/AgNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CDs/AgNPs was carried out at room temperature through a chemical reduction process of AgNO3 employing CDs. In a 100.0 mL volumetric flask, 6.0 mL of the previously prepared CDs’ solution, 2.0 mL of 0.01 M AgNO3, and 1.0 mL of 0.0625 M NH3 were added and diluted to the mark using deionized water. The reaction mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 10.0 min. The resultant yellow solution of CDs/AgNPs was stable when stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for over a month. The procedure for the preparation of CDs/AgNPs is presented in Fig. 2 .

The examination of the prepared CDs/AgNPs was conducted using a Thermo Fisher Scientific transmission electron microscope (TEM, Waltham, USA)). A 200-mesh carbon-coated Cu-grid was utilized for sample examination, which was carried out at a voltage of 200 kV. For Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) an ALPHA II instrument manufactured by Bruker in Germany was used to perform the procedure.

A stock solution of KTF at a concentration of 100.0 µg mL−1 was prepared by dissolving 10.0 mg of KTF in 100.0 mL ethanol. Working solutions were prepared by further dilution of the stock solution using the same solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the determination of KTF
ce:section.ce:para: In a 10-mL volumetric flask, 3.5 mL of CDs/AgNPs solution and 600.0 μL of 0.12 M Britton-Robinson buffer solution (pH 4.0) were added. After the addition of varying amounts of KTF standard, over the range of 3.0–40.0 µg mL−1. The final volume of the combination was adjusted to 10.0 mL using distilled water. Then, the absorbance values at 410 nm were instantaneously measured and a calibration curve was constructed.

A specific volume of Orchazid® eye drops equivalent to 10.0 mg of KTF was diluted using distilled water. The resultant solution was further diluted with the same solvent to obtain working solutions with different concentrations covering the linear range. The procedure outlined under “ 2.7. General procedure for the determination of KTF ’’ was then employed to calculate the % recoveries from the previously constructed calibration curve.

Artificial aqueous humor was formulated to mimic the chemical constituents of human aqueous humor. Part I of artificial aqueous humor consisted of 0.395 mg potassium chloride, 7.44 mg sodium chloride, 2.19 mg sodium bicarbonate, and 0.433 mg dibasic sodium phosphate dissolved in water for injection. On the other hand, each mL of Part II contains 3.85 mg calcium chloride dihydrate, 23.0 mg dextrose, 4.6 mg glutathione disulfide, and 5.0 mg magnesium chloride hexahydrate. To prepare the aqueous humor solution, 20 mL of Part II was added to 480 mL of Part I. The pH was then adjusted to 7.4 using sodium hydroxide and/or hydrochloric acid. Then, one milliliter of the prepared aqueous humor was placed into a set of 10 mL volumetric flasks. These flasks were spiked with KTF so that the final drug concentrations fell within the established calibration range. After that, the procedure outlined under “2.7. General procedure for the determination of KTF’’ was executed to determine the % recoveries using the previously constructed calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for KTF sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Several experimental factors, including pH, buffer volume, amount of CDs/AgNPs, and reaction time, were adjusted to achieve the highest sensitivity for the determination of KTF via CDs/AgNPs. The reduction in absorbance intensity (ΔA = A0 − A, where A0 and A indicate the absorbance of CDs/AgNPs at 410 nm in the absence and presence of KTF, respectively) was utilized as the response for optimization.

The effect of pH has been investigated over the range of 3.0–8.0 using Britton-Robinson buffer. As depicted in Fig. S1
(a) [
Electronic supplementary material
], the reaction showed an increase with pH increased and attained a maximum at pH 3.5, remaining constant until reaching a pH of 4.5 and then decreasing beyond pH 4.5. At acidic pH, low absorbance of CDs/AgNPs was obtained owing to the instability of the CDs/AgNPs at highly acidic conditions. Consequently, a buffer solution of pH 4.0 was selected for further study.

The influence of buffer volume has been investigated using different volumes of Britton-Robinson buffer solution in the range of 300.0 µL to 1200.0 µL while maintaining a pH of 4.0. As illustrated in Fig. S1
(b) [
Electronic supplementary material
], the reaction showed an increase with increasing buffer volume, reaching a maximum at 550.0 µL, remaining constant until a volume of 650.0 µL followed by a decrease beyond 650.0 µL. Therefore, a buffer volume of 600.0 µL was chosen for the subsequent investigations.

The effect of varying the amount of CDs/AgNPs on the reduced intensity has been also investigated within the range of 1.0 to 4.0 mL. The highest response was observed with 3.50 mL of CDs/AgNPs as depicted in Fig. S1
(c) [
Electronic supplementary material
]. As a result, the volume of 3.5 mL was determined as an optimal value for future studies.

The influence of reaction time was investigated within the range of 0.0–10.0 min. The reaction was instantaneous, requiring no additional time to complete it as indicated in Fig. S1
(d) [
Electronic supplementary material
].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of colorimetric sensing of KTF
ce:section.ce:para:
The colorimetric sensing mechanism of KTF using the synthesized CDs/AgNPs is based on the aggregation-induced plasmonic effect and the distinctive optical properties of the CDs/AgNPs. Initially, the CDs/AgNPs appeared as a uniformly distributed bright yellowish-brown solution, stabilized by the repelling electrostatic forces caused by the free-OH functional groups. Moreover, the abundant oxygen-containing –OH groups present on the CD’s surface play a significant role in interacting with analytes. When CDs/AgNPs come into contact with KTF, an interaction occurs between the drug molecules and the CDs/AgNPs surface, possibly through the Ag-S link, or through interaction with the –OH groups on the surface of the CDs/AgNPs. This interaction leads to the aggregation of CDs/AgNPs causing an increase in their size and induces changes in their optical properties. This could be observed through the increase in the size of the CDs/AgNPs after the addition of KTF as illustrated in Fig. S2
[
Electronic supplementary material
]. Furthermore, the aggregation of CDs/AgNPs results in the plasmonic coupling of adjacent AgNPs. This coupling phenomenon leads to a redshift in the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak of the CDs/AgNPs. Consequently, the color of the solution switches from yellow to red and a new peak arises at a longer wavelength. Importantly, this color change is observable to the naked eye. These significant findings can be utilized in the development of a novel colorimetric sensor for the detection of KTF.

The developed method was validated according to the International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) [54] for linearity and range, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantitation (LOQ), accuracy, precision, selectivity, and robustness.

Under the aforementioned optimum conditions, the linearity of the method was evaluated at different concentrations (3.0, 5.0, 8.0, 15.0, 20.0, 30.0, and 40.0 µg mL−1). The method was found to exhibit excellent linearity over a concentration range of 3.0–40.0 µg mL−1 with a high correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9996 ( Table 1
).

The derived regression equation is as follows:
Δ A = 0.0015 + 0.0027 C
where ΔΑ is the difference in absorbance signal at 410 nm and C is the concentration of KTF in µg mL−1. As can be noticed from Fig. S3
[
Electronic supplementary material
], the absorbance signals steadily reduced and thus ΔΑ uniformly increased upon the addition of increasing concentrations of KTF.

LOD and LOQ were determined as the lowest concentrations that can be detected and quantitated, respectively [55]. They were calculated from the following equations:
LOD = 3.3 S a / b
LOQ = 10 S a / b
where Sa is the standard deviation of the intercept of the regression line and b is its slope. LOD and LOQ were determined to be 0.981 and 2.973 µg mL−1, respectively ( Table 1 ).

Both the accuracy and precision of the proposed method were evaluated at three concentration levels over the linear range. The intra-day and inter-day precision were evaluated with three concentrations of KTF on the same day and on three consecutive days, respectively. Moreover, the accuracy was evaluated through the % recovery of the nominal concentrations along with the % error. The high % recoveries obtained (98.40 to 100.15 %) with the low % error (<1.5 %) represent good accuracy while the low % relative standard deviation (% RSD < 2.6) suggests the satisfactory precision of the developed method (Table 2 ).

Robustness determines the method's ability to remain unaffected by small variations in experimental conditions, such as changes in pH, buffer volume, or CDs/AgNPs volume. It is evaluated by deliberately introducing small changes to the method parameters and assessing the impact on the results. The excellent robustness of the developed method can be observed in Fig. S1
[
Electronic supplementary material
].

The application of the developed method to analyze KTF in eye drops holds significant importance in the pharmaceutical industry. The accurate determination of KTF concentration in eye drops is crucial for ensuring the quality and consistency of the pharmaceutical product. As a result, pharmaceutical manufacturers can utilize this method to perform routine analysis to verify that the KTF content meets the required specifications, thus, ensuring the efficacy and safety of the eye drops.

On the other hand, monitoring the concentration of KTF in aqueous humor provides valuable information about its distribution and effectiveness within the eye. This information is also essential for pharmacokinetic studies, helping researchers in understanding the drug's absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination in the eye. This contributes to the overall understanding of its pharmacological profile. Furthermore, determining KTF concentration in the aqueous humor aids in assessing the safety and efficacy of the drug. By ensuring adequate drug levels, healthcare professionals can enhance treatment outcomes and thus, improve patients' quality of life.

Consequently, the developed sensor was applied for the determination of KTF levels in both eye drops and aqueous humor samples. The obtained recoveries of different known concentrations of KTF ranged from 96.97 to 104.33 % as illustrated in Table 3 . It is evident from the excellent recovery that the proposed colorimetric sensor has the feasibility and reliability for the analysis of real samples without interference from different matrices (eye drop preservatives, and the components of aqueous humor). This experimental finding demonstrates the enormous potential for the colorimetric KTF sensor for use in quality control and clinical investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the developed method with other reported methods for KTF determination
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method for KTF determination was compared to several reported methods [3,18,23,25] as depicted in Table 4 . Our method revealed several advantages, including high sensitivity, selectivity, rapid analysis, cost-effectiveness, accuracy, and practicality. Firstly, our method exhibited comparable LOD to most reported methods except for the chemiluminescence-based method. Additionally, our method exhibited a wider linear range. The rapid analysis and simplicity in the preparation of the CDs/AgNPs is another merit of the developed method. These characteristics render it a valuable tool for quantifying KTF in various matrices. As a result, it enables effective drug monitoring, facilitates pharmacokinetic studies, and helps in the management of ophthalmic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comprehensive evaluation of the method greenness and blueness
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the sustainability of analytical methods is crucial for understanding their environmental and economic impacts [56]. However, sustainability is a complex concept that encompasses multiple dimensions, including greenness, waste minimization, safety, performance, and cost considerations. No single tool can comprehensively assess sustainability across all relevant parameters [57]. Therefore, this study adopts a combined multi-tool approach to enable a more holistic evaluation from different complementary perspectives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the method's greenness profile using analytical Eco-Scale, ComplexGAPI, and AGREEprep tools
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the method’s greenness, the Analytical eco-scale, Complex GAPI, and AGREEprep tools were utilized. This allows for a multidimensional sustainability evaluation encompassing critical aspects of greenness from both qualitative and quantitative perspectives.

Firstly, the analytical eco-scale is used for assessing the greenness [58]. It is a scoring system that assesses the greenness of the analytical method based on various environmental criteria such as the use of hazardous substances, energy consumption, waste generation, and the overall environmental impact. It assigns penalty points to different aspects of the method. These penalty points are then subtracted from a base of 100 to give an overall rating or score that indicates the method's level of greenness. Our method has a score of 77 confirming its excellent greenness [Fig. 5
(a)]. However, the eco-scale does not cover the whole aspects of the analytical method.

Consequently, ComplexGAPI [59] has gained widespread recognition and acceptance within the chemical society for its ability to enhance the existing GAPI metric [60]. It achieves this by incorporating an additional hexagonal field, enabling a comprehensive evaluation of all stages of the analytical method, including sample collection, preservation, transportation, preparation, storage, and final analysis. A color-coded scale is utilized to generate a pictogram that provides a comprehensive assessment of the level of greenness at each stage, ranging from green to yellow to red. Notably, ComplexGAPI offers user-friendly shareware software, making it accessible and easy to produce ComplexGAPI pictograms. In our presented method, exemplary greenness is demonstrated by the predominance of green color [Fig. 5 (b)]. This highlights the superiority of our developed method in terms of greenness. However, it should be noted that ComplexGAPI primarily focuses on environmental criteria and does not address other important dimensions such as waste prevention, energy efficiency, and the use of renewable materials. Therefore, it is recommended to combine ComplexGAPI with additional quantitative tools for a more comprehensive assessment.

AGREEprep stands out for its comprehensive nature [61] as it encompasses all twelve principles of Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC). It offers flexibility by allowing the assignment of weights to different parameters, generates a clear and easily understandable pictogram as a result, and can be conveniently applied using freely available software. The final score derived from the combination of these parameters ranges from zero to one. The overall sustainability level of a method can be visually represented using a clock-like graph, where the color and score at the center indicate its overall greenness. A score of one corresponds to a dusky green color, while a score of zero represents a deep red shade. According to the evaluation using AGREEprep, our method demonstrates outstanding greenness, achieving a high score of 0.7, indicating its superior performance in terms of green principles [Fig. 5 (c)]. However, it's important to note that AGREEprep primarily focuses on environmental criteria related to green chemistry principles. It does not address other crucial dimensions of sustainability such as safety, performance, and cost-effectiveness. Therefore, it is recommended to combine AGREEprep with complementary tools that assess other sustainability dimensions to obtain a more comprehensive assessment. Overall, AGREEprep is a valuable tool, but it should be part of a combined approach to guide the advancement of greener and more sustainable analytical techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the method's blueness based on BAGI tool
ce:section.ce:para: While evaluation tools such as eco-scale, ComplexGAPI, and AGREEprep assess the environmental greenness of analytical methods, a recently introduced tool called BAGI focuses specifically on the functionality and applicability of these methods, termed as “blueness” in the context of the study [62]. BAGI enables quantitative assessment of blueness based on ten practical criteria, including analysis type, number of analytes, instrumentation, sample throughput, preparation needs, analysis rate, reagents/materials, preconcentration needs, automation extent, and sample quantity. Each criterion is scored on a scale from 1 (worst) to 10 (best), and the composite BAGI index is calculated as the geometric mean. In this study, BAGI was employed to evaluate and benchmark the blueness of the proposed method. Our method demonstrated a good BAGI score of 67.5, indicating excellent real-world applicability, and cost-effectiveness.

Firstly, the type of analysis, represented by the dark blue color, provides a higher value because it is a quantitative and confirmatory analysis, earning it 10 points. However, the second parameter, the number of analytes received the lowest score of 2.5 points due to the analysis being limited to a single analyte. Although this goes against the principle of Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC), it does demonstrate a high selectivity of the method. As the method is validated using a UV–visible method, the third score is 7.5, indicating the availability of simple instrumentation. Furthermore, the ability to prepare samples simultaneously on different samples earned the method 5 points. Since no sample preparation is required, only a simple dilution of the real samples, the method received a perfect score of 10 points in this parameter. The analysis of a sample using the validated UV–Visible spectrophotometer method takes less than a minute, justifying the assignment of 10 points for the sixth parameter. The CDs/AgNPs used in the study were easily synthesized in the lab using common instruments, and the types and cost of reagents and materials were considered, resulting in 5 points. Regarding the requirement of preconcentration, the method does not necessitate it, earning it the maximum score of 10 points. The absence of common instruments led to the manual treatment and analysis of samples, resulting in 2.5 points for the penultimate parameter. Lastly, for the amount of sample used, 5 points were assigned, as approximately one mL of real samples are typically employed. The BAGI pictogram, with the selection of a single criterion, is depicted in Fig. 5 (d).

To summarize, integrating BAGI with other greenness metrics offers a comprehensive and robust evaluation of sustainability, overcoming the limitations of relying on a single tool. This systems-thinking approach, which utilizes multiple complementary tools, is considered a best practice for conducting thorough and unbiased assessments of the sustainability of analytical methods.

In this study, a simple and highly effective colorimetric sensor was developed for the rapid detection of KTF in eye drops and aqueous humor. This sensor utilizes carbon dots-adorned silver nanoparticles, which demonstrate excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward KTF detection. The sensor's performance was thoroughly evaluated through a series of experiments and characterization techniques. The results revealed that the sensor exhibited a significant color change in the presence of KTF, allowing its colorimetric detection within a short time frame. Furthermore, the colorimetric response showed a linear relationship with KTF concentrations over a range of 3.0–40.0 µg mL−1 and a low LOD of 0.981 µg mL−1, enabling its quantitative analysis. The proposed method was subjected to a comprehensive evaluation of its sustainability, employing various tools such as the analytical eco-scale, ComplexGAPI, AGREEprep, and BAGI. This thorough assessment confirmed the exceptional level of environmental friendliness and resource conservation achieved by the method, as well as its analytical reliability and practical efficiency. The newly developed sensor demonstrates simplicity and cost-effectiveness through the utilization of readily available materials and the requirement of minimal sample preparation, making it well-suited for practical applications in quality control and clinical investigations.
This research is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phar-23–40) .

Aya Magdy Saad: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Asmaa Kamal El-Deen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jenny Jeehan Mohamed Nasr: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124204.

ce:section.ce:para:
